title: Implement TDummyFirestoreApi write overrides with mutation fan-out
blocks: Batch/transaction task — it will build on the same create/update/delete primitives.

status_instructions.on_followup: If write semantics revealed a subtlety the batch/transaction task must honour, append to that task's `side_context`.

description: Override `createDoc`, `updateDoc`, `deleteDoc` (converter + non-converter forms exist on the real api — override what exists). Writes validate the writeable, apply timestamps via the real api's `TTimestampType.add`, mutate `_store` as raw json, and re-emit through `_emitCollections()` and `_emitDoc()` instantly. Creates return a stub `_TDummyDocumentReference` bound to the dummy api (the full stub class is a later task — for now, emit an absolutely minimal placeholder that implements enough of `DocumentReference` to return from `createDoc`). Updates shallow-merge the writeable's json onto the stored map. Deletes remove the map entry.
context_for_junior: Writes are the second half of 'does this feature actually work?'. Every service in the monorepo calls `createDoc`, `updateDoc`, or `deleteDoc` through the api — your overrides must mimic Firestore's partial-update semantics (shallow merge) so the UI optimistic-update paths behave identically. Critical detail: the dummy store holds raw `Map<String, dynamic>`, not typed `T`. Writes update the map; reads re-materialise a typed `T` by piping the stored map through the caller's `fromJson`. That is the trick that lets partial update DTOs (which have many nullable fields) work without crashing — you never try to build a half-filled typed entity from a partial writeable.
why_it_matters: Without correct write semantics the dummy diverges from Firestore's partial-update model and every caller using update DTOs gets either crashes or missing-field bugs in dummy mode.
instructions: Override `createDoc`, `updateDoc`, `deleteDoc` on TDummyFirestoreApi<T>. Match parameter lists exactly to the parent class — after phase 1 they are all instance methods so overriding them compiles.

Every write begins with `await _applyLatency(); final failure = _rollFailureException(operationType: TOperationType.write, path: _pathSnapshot, id: effectiveId, documentData: writeable.toJson()); if (failure != null) return TurboResponse.fail(error: failure);`.

`createDoc`: (1) call `writeable.validate()` and return its failure if it fails; (2) compute the effective id from the caller's supplied `id` or `_genDummyId()`; (3) take the writeable's json and apply timestamps via the existing `TTimestampType.add(...)` using the parent class's `createdAtFieldName` and `updatedAtFieldName`; (4) inject `idFieldName` with the effective id; (5) write the json to `_store[effectiveId]`; (6) call `_emitCollections()` and `_emitDoc(effectiveId, _materialise(_store[effectiveId]!))`; (7) return `TurboResponse.success` with a minimal `_TDummyDocumentReference` carrying the dummy api reference and the id — the full stub class ships in a later task, for now a tiny private placeholder implementing only `.id`, `.path`, and throwing UnimplementedError on everything else is acceptable if the phase-7 task will replace it.

`updateDoc`: (1) look up `_store[id]`; if absent, return `TurboResponse.fail` with a `TurboFirestoreNotFoundException` constructed with the same context the real api produces; (2) call `writeable.validate()`; (3) take `writeable.toJson()`, apply timestamps via `TTimestampType.add`, shallow-merge the result onto the stored map (overlay: stored keys are overwritten by writeable keys; stored keys not in writeable are preserved); (4) write the merged map back; (5) `_emitCollections()` and `_emitDoc(id, _materialise(merged))`; (6) return `TurboResponse.success` with the same `_TDummyDocumentReference` stub.

If the parent class also exposes `createDocInBatch`, `updateDocInBatch`, `deleteDocInBatch`, `createDocInTransaction`, `updateDocInTransaction`, `deleteDocInTransaction` — override those too but leave their bodies throwing `UnimplementedError('wired by the batch/transaction task')`. The batch/transaction task will replace these bodies.
Mutation fan-out is instant — no latency gate on re-emission, matching the spec's `latency_scope.subsequent_stream_emissions` rule.
Run `make analyze package=turbo_firestore_api` and the new unit tests.
goals: Writes mutate the in-memory store, validate writeables, apply timestamps, and fan out through the stream registries.
Partial updates behave like Firestore's shallow merge.
Create returns a usable minimal DocumentReference stub.
Delete nullifies every open doc-stream listener for the deleted id.
non_goals: No batch / transaction semantics — later task.
No full DocumentReference implementation — later task.
acceptance_criteria: Unit test: createDoc writes the entity to the store and open collection streams receive the updated snapshot instantly.
Unit test: updateDoc with a partial writeable (only `name` set) preserves other fields and overwrites `name`.
Unit test: updateDoc on an unknown id returns `TurboResponse.fail` wrapping `TurboFirestoreNotFoundException`.
Unit test: deleteDoc removes the entry and any open doc stream for that id emits `null`.
Unit test: two open collection streams both receive the snapshot after a createDoc — no drift.
Unit test: with `randomFailurePercentage = 100`, writes return TurboResponse.fail without mutating the store.
Unit test: createdAt is set from TTimestampType.add on the first write, updatedAt is updated on subsequent update writes.
constraints: Shallow merge only — no recursive merge, matching Firestore's `update` semantics.
No workaround for validation — if validate() fails, return its failure unchanged.
Instant re-emission on mutation; never await the latency gate after the initial write latency.
All batch/transaction overrides that remain unimplemented throw `UnimplementedError` with a message pointing to the batch/transaction task.
tools: RepoPrompt MCP for reading the real TFirestoreApi write methods (createDoc/updateDoc/deleteDoc) to match parameters, validation, and timestamp handling

testing.rules: No mocks. Use a synthetic DTO with a matching writeable (both create and update variants) defined inline in the test file. Assert on store contents (via a `@visibleForTesting` getter) and on emitted stream snapshots.
scenarios: Given an empty store, When createDoc is called with a writeable, Then the store has one entry with the writeable's fields plus timestamps and id.
Given a store with one entity and an open collection stream, When createDoc adds a second entity, Then the stream emits a two-element snapshot within one microtask.

Given an existing entity, When updateDoc is called with a partial writeable setting only one field, Then the stored map has the original fields merged with the updated field and the updatedAt timestamp is newer.
Given an unknown id, When updateDoc is called, Then the result is TurboResponse.fail wrapping TurboFirestoreNotFoundException.
Given an existing id with an open doc stream, When deleteDoc is called, Then the stream emits null.
Given `randomFailurePercentage = 100`, When createDoc is called, Then the store is unchanged and the result is TurboResponse.fail.
Given two open collection streams, When createDoc fires, Then both streams receive the identical new snapshot.
testing.refactor_for_testability: If asserting on timestamp equality flakes because of wall-clock nondeterminism, inject a `DateTime Function()` clock into the dummy api as an optional constructor parameter (default `DateTime.now`) and flow it through `_applyTimestamps`. Update the spec alignment in `side_context` of the skeleton task.

quality_bar.maintainability: Write preamble (latency + failure roll + validation) is hoisted into one private helper.
quality_bar.dry: No duplicated merge/serialize code across create/update/delete — use the same helpers.
quality_bar.single_responsibility: Writes mutate state + fan out; stream management is untouched.
quality_bar.performance: O(store size) for snapshot re-emission, which is intrinsic to Firestore semantics.
quality_bar.abuse_resistance: validate() is invoked on every writeable path — callers cannot slip through malformed inputs. NotFound errors are typed so UI pattern-matching stays sound.
